segments.s583-g41: نه ... و نه is a correlative negative construction meaning 'neither ... nor'. Here the pattern is: negator + noun + past copula + adjective (نه موبد بود شاد) followed by the second coordinated noun (و نه پهلوان). The verb بود (past of بودن) agrees with the plural/collective sense of the coordinated subjects and yields 'there was no X'.
segments.s583-g42: This begins with the second negative particle نه introducing a clause about 'او' (he). در جهان is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the world' placed before the predicate. شاد روشن‌روان is a compound adjective phrase ('happy, bright‑souled') following the copula which is omitted here (understood from earlier بود); the literal order is 'nor he in the world happy bright‑souled'.
segments.s583-g43: Subject (همه موبدان) precedes the locative phrase نزد شاه and the past plural verb آمدند. نزد + noun means 'in the presence of' or 'before' (a court). آمدند is past plural 'they came'.
segments.s583-g44: Two adjectives/epithets (گشاده‌دل و نیک‌خواه) describe the subject; they are placed before the repeated verb آمدند, which here reiterates that they 'came' and qualifies how they came (i.e. with generous hearts and well‑wishing). The coordinating و links the two descriptive terms.
segments.s583-g45: بگفتند is the past plural 'they said' introducing reported speech; کاین is a poetic contraction of 'که این' ('that this'). The predicate برمنش ('of good disposition' / 'noble‑minded') follows the noun کودک as a predicative adjective describing the child.
segments.s583-g46: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and combines with بیغاره (an archaic/obscure term here left untranslated as a lexical item) plus دورست 'is distant' to mean 'he is far from بیغاره'. The second coordination 'و ز سرزنش' repeats the same preposition z + noun to mean 'and from reproach'. Word order places the prepositional phrases after the adjective they modify.
segments.s583-g47: The subject جهان comes first, followed by the adverbial سربسر meaning 'entirely' or 'throughout'. زیر فرمان اوست is a prepositional phrase 'under his command' with اوست (copula) marking state: 'is under his command'. The sentence follows standard Persian subject‑adverb‑predicate order.
segments.s583-g48: به هر کشوری is a distributive locative phrase 'in/for every country/realm'. باژ و پیمان form a coordinated nominal phrase ('cry/shout and covenant') and اوست ties them to the ruler—literally 'the cry and covenant are his' or 'belong to him/are in force under him'. The copula is singular here, serving to ascribe possession/state to the compound noun phrase.
segments.s583-g57: هم‌انگه is a poetic adverb meaning 'at that moment' placed before the verb for emphasis; فرستاد is the simple past 'he sent/dispatched' and governs the following object کسها ('people/persons'), while the prepositional phrase به روم ('to Rome') gives the destination.
segments.s583-g58: A paratactic list of destinations introduced by the preposition به repeated for poetic rhythm; آباد بوم is an adjective + noun compound meaning 'prosperous/inhabited land' (آباد modifies بوم).
segments.s583-g59: همان 'that' qualifies نامداری ('renown' used here as a nominal label = 'the renowned one'); سوی تازیان is a directional prepositional phrase 'toward the Arabs' placed after the noun in typical Persian poetic order.
segments.s583-g60: بشد is a classical past form of شدن used narratively ('he went/it came about'); تا introduces a purpose clause 'so that/in order that' and is followed by ببین د (ببیند) in the subjunctive sense 'that he may see'; به سود و زیان is the object phrase 'as to gain and loss' (advantages and disadvantages).
segments.s583-g61: به هر سو 'to every direction/ everywhere' is an adverbial phrase; همی before the past verb رفت is the classical continuous/habitual marker (so همی رفت = 'kept going' / 'went about'); خواننده‌ای is an indefinite noun 'a reciter' functioning as the subject after the verb in poetic word order.
segments.s583-g62: که is a relative pronoun linking to خواننده‌ای ('who/that'); بهرام را with را marks Bahram as the definite object of پروراننده‌ای ('a nurturer/fosterer of Bahram'), the predicate which describes that reciter.
segments.s583-g63: بجوید is a present/narrative verb 'he sought' (used as the main verb of the line); the nouns سخنگوی and دانش‌پذیر are coordinated objects of that verb, 'a speaker/orator and a teachable student,' with و simply linking them.
segments.s583-g64: This continues the list of sought qualities: سخن‌دان 'an eloquent person' and then هر دانشی یادگیر is an idiomatic noun phrase 'one who learns any/ every knowledge' (هر دانشی 'any knowledge' + یادگیر 'learner'), with the verb from the previous segment understood to govern these items as well.
segments.s583-g97: ستاره‌شمر (compound noun) = 'star‑reader/astrologer'. نیست is the negated existential/predicative verb 'is not / there is not'. چون ما کسی places a likeness after the negation: literally 'there is no astrologer like us (anyone)'. Word order is poetic/inverted but amounts to 'کسی چون ما ستاره‌شمر نیست' ('there is no one who reads the stars like us').
segments.s583-g98: که introduces a relative clause modifying کسی: 'who/that'. از هندسه بهره دارد بسی = 'has a share/benefit from geometry'. دارد is present third‑person singular 'has/possesses'. بسی functions adverbially here ('much / plentifully') and follows the verb in this poetic register.
segments.s583-g99: پر از مهر شاهست = 'is full of the king's love/affection' (مهر + شاه as genitive). شاهست is an archaic/contracted sequence of شاه + است ('is the king's' / 'is [of the] king'), here functioning as 'of the king'. ما را روان = 'our soul' (ما را is topical/definite marking + روان 'soul/spirit'). The clause reads as a predicate applied to 'ما را روان' in a poetic word order: 'Our soul is full of the king's love.'
segments.s583-g100: به زیر اندرون is a prepositional phrase 'into/under the inner (part)' (به + زیر + اندرون). تازی اسپان = 'Arabian horses' (تازی as adjective modifying اسپان). دمان ('دامان', 'hem/flank/edge') is used figuratively to name the flanks or folds associated with those horses. The whole phrase describes location beneath the inner area in relation to the Arabian horses' flanks/folds; word order and some nouns are poetically compacted.
segments.s583-g101: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject. پیش فرزند تو = 'in the presence of / before your son' (prepositional phrase with پیش). بنده‌ایم is first‑person plural present 'we are servants' (بنده as humble self‑designation). The clause follows typical Persian SOV order with the verb at the end.
segments.s583-g102: بزرگی وی را 'his greatness' is the definite object (وی 'he/his', را object marker after it). ستاینده‌ایم is built from the participial stem ستاینده + present plural auxiliary ایم, functioning as 'we praise' or 'we are praisers of' — here a present habitual/performative action.
segments.s583-g89: نگر is an imperative 'look/see' addressed to the addressee. تا introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'until/so that' with the sense of checking result; پسندت is a nominal + enclitic pronoun 'your liking/approval' (پسند + ‑ت). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that it comes to your liking', آید is the present-form 'comes/happens', and همی is an archaic emphatic/continuous particle (classical Persian) that follows the verb to add insistence or continuity.
segments.s583-g90: وگر is a conditional 'if' (or 'but if'); سودمندت is an adjective 'useful/profitable' with the enclitic pronoun ‑ت 'to you'; که + آید repeats the subordinate 'that it comes/happens', and همی again is the archaic emphatic/continuous particle. The whole clause offers an alternative condition: 'or if it will be useful to you.'
segments.s583-g91: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the speech; گفت here is the verb 'said' (classical past) introducing reported speech, but the provided sense list did not include the verbal sense so the appropriate sense index is unavailable; که introduces the content clause 'that...'; ما is the subject pronoun 'we'; بنده‌ایم is the predicative 'we are servants' (بنده as 'servant' used humbly) with the present/imperative copular ending ‑ایم marking first‑person plural.
segments.s583-g92: خود is emphatic 'ourselves' or 'we ourselves'; اندر جهان is a locative prepositional phrase 'in/within the world' (اندر = 'in'); شاه را uses the object/beneficiary marker را to indicate the king as the one for whom they are alive ('for the king'); زنده‌ایم is the present copula + adjective 'we are alive' (first‑person plural). Word order is emphatic: 'we ourselves in the world are alive for the king.'
segments.s583-g93: هنرهای is the plural 'arts/skills' with the possessive relation expressed by the following ما 'our'; شاه is the subject 'the king'; داند is an archaic/polite present form of 'to know' (داند = 'knows'); همه means 'all' and completes the object 'all our arts'. The verb follows the subject in typical classical word order.
segments.s583-g94: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that'; او is the third‑person pronoun 'he'; چون is the simile particle 'like/as'; شبانست is a fused form 'is a shepherd' (شبان + است); و is the coordinating 'and'; ما is 'we'; چون + رمه forms the simile 'we are like a flock.' The clause contrasts the king's role with the people's role using two similes.
segments.s583-g95: سواریم is first‑person plural and here functions as a predicate 'we are horsemen/are mounted' (predicate nominal form). و connects coordinated predicates; گردیم is a present/stative or customary‑action verb 'we roam/turn about' (here 'we roam'); اسپ is the noun 'horse/steed'; افگنیم is a classical verb form with first‑person plural ending indicating action 'we cause/set/produce' (here 'we set in motion' or 'we unleash' regarding horses). The sequence lists habitual collective actions of the speakers.
segments.s583-g96: کسی را is 'someone/anyone' with را marking the object/target; که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'; دانا بود is 'was/is wise' with بود as the past/copular form (here describing the person as wise); بشکنیم is a first‑person plural subjunctive/optative form meaning 'we may break/overthrow/defeat' (the ب‑ prefix here forms the present/subjunctive stem in classical usage). The clause says they will break/defeat anyone who is (or was) wise.
segments.s583-g9: This is a locative prepositional phrase with the subject following: 'به در بر ستاره‌شمر هرک بود' = 'at the door of the astrologer stood whoever'. 'به در' = 'at/to the door' (به + در (door)), while بر here functions as a directional/relational preposition linking در to ستاره‌شمر (the astrologer). هرک is an indefinite relative 'whoever' and بود is the past copula 'was/stood (there)'. Word order is subject (هرک) after the place phrase, typical in epic narrative.
segments.s583-g10: که introduces a relative clause modifying هرک: 'who was fitting/worthy to hear their speech.' شایست is an adjective 'fitting/worthy' that governs گفتار (speech). ایشان refers back to some previously mentioned people ('their/them'), and شنود is the classical third-person form 'he hears' (used here in narrative to mean 'would hear' / 'was able to hear'). The clause as a whole describes the quality that made someone stand at the astrologer's door.
segments.s583-g11: یکی introduces an indefinite 'a certain/one'. مایه‌ور is a predicative adjective 'wealthy/possessing wealth' linked to بود 'was'. با + (فر و هوش) is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with (divine) glory and intelligence' describing qualities he possessed. The sequence is essentially 'There was a certain wealthy man, with glory and intelligence.'
segments.s583-g12: سر هندوان is a title-like NP 'the head/chief of the Indians' (سر = chief/leader). بود is the copula 'was.' نامش = نام + enclitic ش 'his name' (the enclitic attaches to نام), and سروش is the proper name. Word order here is nominal predicate followed by identificational 'نامش ...' giving his name.
segments.s583-g13: یکی پارسی بود = 'there was a certain Persian.' هشیار نام = 'named Hoshiar' (نام follows the name in this order). Although هشیار is semantically 'alert', here it functions as a proper name introduced by نام (i.e. 'whose name was Hoshiar'). The copula بود links the NP and the following name phrase.
segments.s583-g14: که introduces a relative clause describing that Persian. بر چرخ = 'upon the wheel' (چرخ often denotes the sky or the wheel of fate). کردی is a poetic/archaic verbal form here functioning as a past action 'placed/did put' (classical texts sometimes use -ی endings), effectively 'who had placed/put'. به دانش لگام = 'a bridle/reins by/with knowledge' (به here = 'by/with' in an instrumental sense). The whole clause: 'who had put the reins of knowledge upon the wheel.'
segments.s583-g15: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past 'he commanded/said.' تا here functions as a result/sequence marker 'so/and then' leading to the next clause. پیش شاه آمدند = 'they came before the king' (پیش = in the presence of; آمدند is third-person plural past 'they came'). The overall sense: 'He commanded, and so they came before the king.'
segments.s583-g16: A simple coordinated NP subject + verb: 'هشیوار و جوینده راه آمدند' = 'Hoshiwar and the seeker of the way came.' هشیوار is a proper name (here used as a noun), و joins the two NPs, جوینده راه = 'seeker of the way/path', and آمدند is the plural past 'they came.' Word order is canonical Subject–Verb in narrative.
segments.s583-g25: بگفتند is the past plural verb 'they said' (active). با + تاجور یزدگرد means 'with/to the merchant Yazdgerd' (با = with/to; تاجور = merchant; یزدگرد is a personal name). Word order places the indirect object phrase after the verb, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s583-g26: که introduces a reported clause 'that'. دانش ز هرگونه کردیم گرد is a verbal clause: دانش 'knowledge' is the theme, ز هرگونه 'of/from every kind' specifies scope, کردیم is the past plural 'we did/acted', and گرد here functions as a verbal particle meaning 'to go about/seek' (i.e. 'we went about seeking knowledge'). The poetic order places the particle after کردیم.
segments.s583-g27: چنان 'thus/so' modifies آمد (past 'came/arrived'). اندر شمار سپهر is a locative phrase 'into the count/ranks of the sky/heavens' (اندر = in/into; شمار = number/rank; سپهر = heaven). Together: 'thus came into the heavenly ranks.'
segments.s583-g28: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause 'for/that'. دارد is present 'has/holds'. The tokenization shows ب + دین but they form بدین 'to/for this' (the separate token ب is the prepositional prefix 'to/for'); دین here is not used with its literal meaning 'religion' but as part of the demonstrative construction, so the listed senses for دین do not apply. کودک 'child' is the beneficiary, خرد 'wisdom' and مهر 'affection/favour' form the object: 'wisdom has shown favour to this child.'
segments.s583-g29: This line uses an archaic/poetic dative/benefactive mar/مر ('for/to him') followed by او را (he with object marker for emphasis), then بود 'was' and the noun phrase هفت کشور زمین 'seven countries of the earth' as the predicate; literal order: 'For him there was seven countries of the earth.' The initial مر is archaic and not the same as the modern 'me'.
segments.s583-g30: گرانمایه 'precious/esteemed' modifies شاهی 'kingship/royal rule'. بود is the past copula 'was/possessed'. بافرین is an epithet 'praiseworthy/glorious' completing the description of his kingship: 'he had a precious, praiseworthy kingship.'
segments.s583-g31: ز گفتارشان 'from/because of their speech' (ز = from/because; گفتارشان = their words). شاد شد شهریار 'the king became joyful' — شد is the past of شدن 'to become' and شهریار is the subject placed after the predicate for poetic effect.
segments.s583-g32: ببخشیدشان here is a past transitive verb 'he bestowed/gave to them' (classically بخشیدن can mean 'to give' as well as 'to forgive'); ـشان is the 3pl object suffix. گوهر شاهوار 'a regal gem/jewel' is the gift. Note: the provided sense list for ببخشیدشان only shows 'forgive/excuse', but in this context it functions as 'bestowed/gave'.
segments.s583-g65: بیامد is a past third‑person narrative 'came' (here used impersonally). ز is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'from', and هر کشوری is a genitive/possessive phrase 'every country' (کشوری = 'of the country/realm'); موبدی follows the verb as the patient/subject ('a mobed came from every country'). Word order (verb before noun phrase) is normal for this elevated/archaic style.
segments.s583-g66: These three words are predicates/adjectival descriptions of the mobed. جهاندیده ('worldly‑wise') and نیک‌پی ('of good birth/lineage') are descriptive compounds following the noun, linked by و. بخردی is an abstract/qualitative form ('of wisdom' / 'full of wisdom') with the pronominal/qualitative sense; together they characterize the mobed's qualities in the classical, noun‑followed‑by‑attributes order.
segments.s583-g67: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. یکسر here means 'entirely/at once' or 'all together' modifying آمدند. the sequence b + دان represents the older contraction بدان = 'to that' (ب = to/into + دان/آن = 'that'), so بدان بارگاه آمدند = 'they came to that court.' آمدند is the past plural verb 'they came.' This is an archaic phrasing where the prepositional element (بدان) is split in the word list but functions as a single locative 'to that.'
segments.s583-g68: پژوهنده is a noun 'inquirer / seeker / scholar' used collectively here; نزدیک شاه آمدند is a verbal phrase 'they came near the king' with نزدیک serving as an adverb/locative 'near' and شاه the object/goal. The plural verb آمدند agrees with the collective/compound subject implied by پژوهنده.
segments.s583-g69: بپرسید is a past narrative form 'asked' (classical b‑prefix with past stem). بسیار here modifies the verb meaning 'much' or 'many (questions)' — 'asked much' / 'asked many questions.' و links this clause to بنواختشان; بنواختشان is past 'caused to be played/played for them' (نواختن with the object pronoun ‑شان = 'them'), used idiomatically to mean 'entertained/honoured them' by music or festivity.
segments.s583-g70: به هر برزنی is a prepositional phrase 'in/at every برزن' (برزن here = village square / threshing‑floor, i.e. public place). جایگه 'a place/seat/position' is the object and ساختشان ('made for them' / 'appointed them') is the past verb with the object pronoun ‑شان. The clause thus means he provided/appointed them places in every locality; verb at the end reflects classical Persian SOV order.
segments.s583-g71: برفتند is the past plural 'they departed/went away' (بر‑ prefix + رفتند). The proper names نعمان و منذر follow the verb (verb‑first order is acceptable in classical Persian). به شب is a temporal phrase 'at/by night.' The whole segment reads 'Nu'man and Mundhir departed at night.'
segments.s583-g72: This line states that many famous men gathered/assembled. بسی is the classical adverb 'very/many' (here 'many'); نامداران = 'renowned ones' modifies the following idea; گرد is an older verbal/adverbial form meaning 'gathered/around' (i.e. they assembled), and از عرب = 'from the Arabs' marks their origin. The verb is compressed into style (classical usage) so the clause reads 'many famed men from the Arabs assembled.'
segments.s583-g49: نگه کن is an imperative: نگه (here 'look' or 'take heed') + کن (imperative 'do'), telling the hearer to look or consider. به جایی introduces a prepositional phrase 'to/at a place'; که begins a relative clause 'where/that' modifying جایی, and دانش بود is a past-tense clause 'there was knowledge' (بود = past copula). Word order is typical poetic Persian: imperative + prepositional phrase + relative clause.
segments.s583-g50: ز here functions causally ('because of'/'on account of') introducing the reason. داننده کشور is a genitive construction 'a knower/expert of the realm' (داننده governing کشور). به رامش بود means 'was in/at rest/tranquility' (بود = was); به is the preposition linking the state رامش. The whole clause gives the reason why the place was at peace.
segments.s583-g51: ز introduces source or selection 'from' (poetic). پرمایگان is the group 'the nobles/great ones' (object of selection). دایگانی is the role to be chosen ('cauldron-keepers' / kitchen stewards). گزین is an imperative 'choose' (verb placed after the object in this terse poetic instruction).
segments.s583-g52: که begins a purpose/result clause 'so that' or 'in order that'. باشد is the present-subjunctive/jussive form 'may be' or 'let there be'. ز کشور is a prepositional phrase 'from the country/realm'. برو آفرین functions as a verbal phrase meaning 'praise go forth' — برو (go) + آفرین (acclamation/praise), i.e. 'that praise may go out from the land.'
segments.s583-g53: The clause begins with the object/abstract noun هنر ('skill, art'). گیرد is third-person singular present/subjunctive form of گرفتن here meaning 'acquires' or 'takes up.' این شاه خرم is the subject phrase 'this joyful king'; نهان ('secretly') is an adverb modifying گیرد. Poetic word order places the object before the verb and the adverb after the subject.
segments.s583-g54: ز فرمان او is an instrumental/causal phrase 'by his command' (ز = because of/on account of). شاد گردد جهان is inverted word order: گردد ('becomes') with predicate شاد ('happy, glad') governing subject جهان ('the world') — 'the world becomes glad.' گردد is the present/subjunctive/optative form used narratively for 'becomes.'
segments.s583-g55: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when.' بشنید is simple past 'he heard.' زان موبدان is 'that/from those mobeds' (زان = 'from that / from them'), indicating the content/source of what was heard. یزدگرد (placed at the end) is the subject 'Yazdegerd' — poetic inversion places verb before subject.
segments.s583-g56: ز کشور marks origin/extent 'throughout/from the country.' فرستادگان is the object 'envoys/messengers.' کرد is the past of کردن 'he made/sent' (causative/do), and گرد here functions adverbially 'around/about' (i.e. sent around the land). The clause therefore means he sent envoys throughout the realm.
segments.s583-g1: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'; شاهیش (شاه + -یش) means 'his kingship/reign' and comes after the preposition. بگذشت is a past-tense verb meaning 'passed' used impersonally of time. چون هفت سال is a duration phrase 'as/seven years' (چون here = 'as/for'), so the whole segment reads 'seven years passed of his reign.'
segments.s583-g2: همه موبدان is the subject 'all the priests'; زو is the contracted poetic preposition + pronoun 'از او' meaning 'because of him/from him'. به introduces the resulting condition ('to/into suffering and burden'). This clause therefore means 'all the priests, because of him, were a source of suffering and burden.'
segments.s583-g3: سر here means 'the start/beginning (of)'; سال هشتم is 'the eighth year'; مه is the archaic/poetic form related to 'ماه' (month/moon) and is followed by فرودین (Farvardin, the month). The phrase gives a precise temporal point: 'at the beginning of the eighth year, in the month Farvardin.'
segments.s583-g4: که introduces a relative/temporal clause ('when/that'); پیدا کند is a causative/active formation meaning 'appears/is made visible' with کند as the present/active verb. در جهان is the prepositional locative 'in the world'; هور دین functions as a proper name (Hūr-dīn) and is the thing that 'appears in the world.'
segments.s583-g5: یکی کودک is 'a certain/a single child' (یکی here = 'a certain'); آمدش is 'came to him' with the clitic -ش = 'to him' and functions here as 'was born to him' or 'came into his household'; هرمزد روز is a temporal phrase 'on Hormazd day' (هرمزد used as a proper name/name of the day) with روز 'day'.
segments.s583-g6: به introduces the accompanying circumstance ('with' or 'under'); نیک اختر و فال = 'auspicious star and omen' (نیک = auspicious here). گیتی فروز is a descriptive compound where فروز ('bright/illuminating') qualifies the omen as 'world-illuminating' or 'that lit the world.' The whole phrase characterizes the child's auspicious birth.
segments.s583-g7: هم‌آنگه is an adverbial 'immediately/at that same time'; پدر is the subject 'the father'; کرد is the past-tense verb 'did/made' used in the idiom 'نام ... کرد' meaning 'named (him) ...'; بهرام نام is the object phrase showing the name given. Poetic word order places the object after the verb.
segments.s583-g8: ازان کودک is 'that child' (ازان = 'from that/of that'); خرد شد is 'became wise' where خرد is an adjectival/nominal root 'wisdom/wise' and شد is the past 'became'; شادکام 'fortunate/happy' is a second predicate describing the resulting state. The clause means 'that child became wise and happy/fortunate.'
segments.s583-g81: This is an enumerative prepositional phrase. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing a list ('from Rûm and India'), with و linking items. A second preposition از appears before پارسی to repeat 'from' for the Persian item (a stylistic/poetic repetition). Word order is preposition + place name; the whole phrase lists origins/kinds of scholars.
segments.s583-g82: نجومی here is a noun/adjective used as a noun meaning 'astronomer/astrologer'. The conjunction وگر (classical/often written و گر) introduces an alternative or condition 'and if / but if', leading into a verbless clause where the copula is omitted (common in poetry). مردم هندسی = 'people of geometry' i.e. 'geometers'; هندسی is an adjectival form 'geometric' modifying مردم.
segments.s583-g83: همه is a universal quantifier 'all/every', placed before the noun فیلسوفان 'philosophers'. بسیاردان is a compound adjective 'very learned' placed after the noun (postnominal adjective), a common pattern in Persian poetry.
segments.s583-g84: سخن‌گوی functions as an adjectival attribute here ('eloquent' or 'one who speaks well') applied to the same group; وز is a poetic contraction (effectively و + از) meaning 'and (from)', linking to the next descriptive phrase — this is an archaic/poetic connective rather than the literal nouns listed in the senses. مردم کاردان = 'the people who are skilled' (مردم 'people' + postnominal adjective کاردان 'skilled').
segments.s583-g85: بگفتند is the past tense third‑person plural 'they said'. هریک 'each (one)' is the subject (each of them). به آواز نرم is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'in a soft/gentle voice' where به + noun ('آواز') marks the manner and نرم is an adverbial adjective 'gently/soft'.
segments.s583-g86: که‌ای here introduces the direct address and should be read as poetic 'که ای' = 'O' (vocative) before شاه 'king' (the provided senses don't list the vocative use, so it's treated as a poetic compound). باداد is an archaic jussive/optative form meaning 'grant' or 'may (you) give' addressed to the king. The sequence و با رای و شرم is 'and with counsel and (with) modesty' — با is the instrumental 'with', رای here means 'counsel/opinion', and شرم 'modesty/decency'.
segments.s583-g87: همه 'all' (here emphatic) + سربسر ('entirely' / 'throughout') is an adverbial intensifier. خاک پای توایم is a poetic genitival phrase 'the dust of your feet, I am' with the ezāfe omitted (typical in classical Persian). توایم is the fused form 'تو + ایم' meaning 'I am yours' or 'I am at/for you' — idiomatically 'I am the dust of your feet' = 'I am utterly humble before you.'
segments.s583-g88: به دانش is an adverbial phrase 'in/with respect to knowledge' (به marking domain or manner). همه modifies the following noun phrase 'رهنمای' (guide/adviser) producing 'in all branches of learning, (we are) your guides'; توایم again is the predicate 'I am yours' (first‑person copula fused to the possessor). The verb 'am' is expressed by توایم, so the clause reads 'I am your guide in all knowledge.'
segments.s583-g17: به صلاب کردند ز اختر نگاه is a compact, somewhat archaic poetic phrasing: کردند is a past plural verb 'they did/made'; به صلاب is an adverbial phrase ‘with firmness’ (به + noun used adverbially), and ز اختر (literally 'from/of the star') links the source or object of that gaze. Put together: 'they, with firmness, made (directed) a gaze toward the star.'
segments.s583-g18: هم introduces an additional action 'also'; از marks source 'from'; زیچ رومی is a noun phrase 'the Roman zīch' (زیچ an archaic small stringed instrument); بجستند is past plural 'they sought' (here = 'looked/searching for'); and راه is the object 'the way/path' placed after the verb in typical Persian order.
segments.s583-g19: از اختر gives the source 'from the star'; چنان is a degree adverb 'so/such'; دید is the past tense 'he saw/he beheld'; and خرم نهان is a two-word descriptive phrase 'secretly joyful' (خرم = joyful, نهان = hidden/secret). Syntactically this is a typical Persian clause: adverbial/source + degree + verb + adjective phrase as the object/complement.
segments.s583-g20: که introduces a result/explanatory clause 'that'; او is the third‑person pronoun 'he/that one' referring to the person seen; شهریاری here names his status—'sovereignty/kingship' (i.e. 'he was a sovereign'); بود is the past copula 'was'; در جهان is a locative 'in the world'. Together: 'that he was a sovereign in the world.'
segments.s583-g21: ابر هفت کشور is a terse poetic nominal phrase where ابر ('cloud') is used figuratively with هفت کشور ('seven countries/realms'); بود is the past copula 'was'; and پادشا is the predicate noun 'king/monarch'. In plain order: 'He was king of the seven countries.'
segments.s583-g22: گو is an imperative or performative verb 'say/declare'; شاددل is an adjectival phrase 'joyful‑hearted'; باشد is a present/subjunctive/optative form 'may be' or 'let him be' (here giving a wish/characterisation); و connects 'and'; پارسا is an adjective/noun 'pious'. The clause functions as an instruction or commendation: 'Say that he be joyfulhearted and pious.'
segments.s583-g23: برفتند is the past plural 'they went/departed'; پویان is a participial epithet 'the seekers/active ones' modifying the subject; بر + شهریار marks direction 'to/toward the king'. Word order here is verb + epithet + prepositional phrase, a compact poetic arrangement meaning 'the seekers went to the king.'
segments.s583-g24: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same'; زیچ و صلابها is a coordinated noun phrase 'zīch and the salābs' (صلابها is plural); بر کنار is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'at the side/nearby' describing their being at hand. Together this gives 'the same zīch and salābs at (their) side.'
segments.s583-g73: بزرگان is a plural noun meaning 'the great ones / nobles' (plural -ان). چو here is a conjunction meaning 'when' placed after the subject to introduce a temporal clause. در پارس is a prepositional phrase 'in Pars' (location). گرد آمدند is a compound verbal idea: گرد here contributes the sense of 'around/assembly' and آمدند is the past third-person plural 'they came', so the whole phrase means 'they gathered/assembled'.
segments.s583-g74: بر is used as a directional preposition 'toward / to' (here marking the object/goal of coming). تاجور in this context functions as an epithet attached to the king (not the literal 'merchant'), so none of the provided senses fits precisely. یزدگرد is the proper name 'Yazdgerd' (a king). آمدند is the past third-person plural 'they came/went'. Together: 'they came to Yazdgerd (the تاجور)'.
segments.s583-g75: همی is an archaic/poetic particle that gives an iterative or continuous flavour ('used to / kept on / emphatic'), here introducing reported speech. گفت is the verb 'said' (past); the provided senses list it as a noun, so none fit the verb use. هرکس که is a relative/indefinite 'whoever that', introducing the conditional/relative clause. ما بنده‌ایم is present-tense self-humbling formula 'we are (your) servants' where بنده (humble self-reference) + ایم (present 1pl).
segments.s583-g76: سخن is 'speech / words'. بشنویم is the 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive form (prefixed ب-), used optatively/jussively here — 'let (him) speak so that we may hear' or 'we shall hear'. و is the conjunction 'and'. سراینده‌ایم is present 'we are singers/poets' (سراینده + ایم), a descriptive present statement completing their claim.
segments.s583-g77: که is the relative/complementizer 'that/which' linking this clause to the speech. باید is an impersonal modal 'it is necessary / one ought to'. چنین روزگار is the noun phrase 'such a time/condition/behaviour' (چنین = 'such'). از مهان literally 'from/of guests' — the phrase means 'required of guests'. Word order places the impersonal modal before the noun phrase it scopes over.
segments.s583-g78: که again is a relative 'which/that' connecting to what follows. بایسته is an adjective 'fitting, proper' describing what is required. فرزند شاه جهان is a genitive/name phrase 'the son of the king of the world' (noun + possessor phrase); the overall meaning is 'as befits the son of the king of the world.'
segments.s583-g79: به is a preposition 'to/for'. بر گیرد is from the compound verb برگرفتن ('to take up / lift up'); here گیرد (present/archaic jussive/indicative) with the separable prefix بر gives 'take up' or 'raise'. و is 'and'. دانش آموزدش is a verb phrase: دانش 'knowledge' and آموزدش (آموزد + ش) is 3rd-person singular 'he teaches him' (ش = him). In context this is an instruction or hope: 'take him up and teach him knowledge.'
segments.s583-g80: دل از تیرگیها is a noun phrase 'the heart from the darknesses' (دل = heart; از = from/of; تیرگیها plural 'darknesses / gloom'). بیفروزدش is an optative/causative form of فروختن/فروزیدن (here 'to illumine/brighten') with the prefix بی- and enclitic ش = 'him', giving 'may he illuminate him' or 'he will brighten him'. The clause means 'and brighten his heart out of darkness.'
segments.s583-g33: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. ایشان is the third-person plural pronoun 'they'; برفتند is a past-tense, third-person plural verb 'they departed/went away'. زان is an archaic prepositional/locative element 'from that' (زان = از + آن) and بارگاه is the governed noun 'court/palace'; the order is conjunction → subject pronoun → verb → prepositional phrase.
segments.s583-g34: This is a nominal list joined by و (and): رد و موبد و پاک دستور شاه. Each item is a noun or noun phrase coordinated; دستور شاه = 'the king's dastur' (dastur here is a title/office or 'order' depending on context). The phrase is elliptical (no finite verb) and functions as a following apposition or enumeration after the preceding clause.
segments.s583-g35: نشستند و جستند are two past-tense verbs joined by و: نشستن 'to sit' (here 'they sat [down to deliberate]') and جستن in the sense 'to seek' (they sought). هرگونه is a determinative phrase 'every kind / of every sort' modifying رای 'counsel/opinion/plan'—the noun follows the determiner in usual Persian word order.
segments.s583-g36: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that') and تا here functions as a purposive/goal marker 'so that / in order that'. چاره 'remedy/solution' is the direct object; آن 'that' points back to the matter under discussion; چه is the interrogative/indefinite 'what' and آید is the present/subjunctive form of آمدن 'to come' used here for potential/future ('should come / will come'). به جای literally 'into place' = 'take effect / come to pass.'
segments.s583-g37: گرین is a compact/poetic conditional form (essentially 'گر این' = 'if this'); کودک 'child' is the noun; خرد here functions adjectivally as 'small/little' (i.e. 'little child'); خوی 'disposition/character' and پدر 'father' form the genitive phrase 'his father's disposition'. The clause up to this point sets the protasis ('if...' condition).
segments.s583-g38: نَگیرد is the present/subjunctive/negative of گرفتن used here as 'does not take' (i.e. 'does not adopt/receive'). شو is the imperative 'become'; خسروی is an epithet meaning 'kingly / a king' and دادگر is an epithet 'just / just-ruling', so the sequence is an exhortation: 'become a just king' (imperative + nominal predicate).
segments.s583-g39: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is a small archaic/poetic particle meaning roughly 'in that case / by chance / if so' (it nuances the condition). خوی پدر 'the father's disposition' is the object of دارد 'has/possesses'; اوی is the emphatic/old third‑person pronoun 'he' (postposed). The verb دارد is present indicative 'has'.
segments.s583-g40: همه 'all/whole' modifies بوم 'land, realm'. زیر و زبر is a paired antonymic phrase 'below and above' (i.e. 'under and over' / 'throughout the realm'); دارد اوی again 'he has/possesses' with the pronoun اوی placed after the verb for emphasis. The overall meaning: he holds the entire domain above and below.

sentences.s583-s21: There was neither a joyful mobed nor a champion; nor was he in the world happy and bright‑souled.
sentences.s583-s22: All the mobeds came before the king; they came generous‑hearted and well‑wishing.
sentences.s583-s23: They said that this child was of noble disposition, far from bi-ghāreh and free from reproach.
sentences.s583-s24: The whole world, throughout, is under his command; in every realm his cry and his covenant are (in force)/belong to him.
sentences.s583-s29: At that moment he dispatched people to Rome, to India, to China, and to prosperous lands.
sentences.s583-s30: That same renowned one turned toward the Arabs so that he might see the profit and the loss.
sentences.s583-s31: Everywhere there went a reciter — one who had nurtured Bahram.
sentences.s583-s32: He sought a speaker and a receptive student, an eloquent man and one who would learn every sort of knowledge.
sentences.s583-s49: There is no star‑reader like us — no one who possesses much knowledge of geometry.
sentences.s583-s50: Our soul is full of the king's love; beneath the inner (court) are the folds/flanks of the Arabian horses.
sentences.s583-s51: We all are servants before your son; we are the praisers of his greatness.
sentences.s583-s45: See whether it will please you, or whether it will be useful to you.
sentences.s583-s46: Thus Munḏir said that we are servants; we ourselves, in the world, are alive for the king.
sentences.s583-s47: The king knows all our skills, for he is like a shepherd and we are like a flock.
sentences.s583-s48: We are horsemen and we roam and we set our steeds in motion; any person who is wise, we shall break (defeat).
sentences.s583-s5: At the astrologer's door stood whoever was fit to hear their speech.
sentences.s583-s6: There was a certain wealthy man, endowed with glory and wit; he was the chief of the Indians, his name Soroush.
sentences.s583-s7: There was a Persian, named Hoshiar, who had put the reins of knowledge upon the wheel (of fate).
sentences.s583-s8: He ordered it; they came before the king — Hoshiwar and the seeker of the way came.
sentences.s583-s13: They told the merchant Yazdgerd that we had gone about acquiring knowledge of every kind.
sentences.s583-s14: Thus he entered the heavenly ranks, for wisdom showed favour to this child.
sentences.s583-s15: To him belonged seven countries of the earth; he possessed a precious, praiseworthy kingship.
sentences.s583-s16: From their words the king became joyful; he bestowed upon them a regal jewel.
sentences.s583-s33: From every country came a mobed — worldly-wise, of good descent, and full of wisdom.
sentences.s583-s34: When they all at once came to that court, the inquirers drew near the king.
sentences.s583-s35: He asked many questions and entertained them; he appointed them places (posts) in every village‑square.
sentences.s583-s36: Nu'man and Mundhir departed by night; many famed men from the Arabs assembled.
sentences.s583-s25: Look to the place where there was learning; because of a knowing ruler the realm was at peace.
sentences.s583-s26: Choose cauldron-keepers from the nobles, so that praise may go forth from the country.
sentences.s583-s27: This joyful king secretly acquires skill; by his command the world becomes glad.
sentences.s583-s28: When Yazdegerd heard that from those mobeds, he dispatched envoys throughout the land.
sentences.s583-s1: Seven years passed of his reign, and all the mōbeds became a source of hardship and burden to him.
sentences.s583-s2: At the start of the eighth year, in the month Farvardin, when Hūr-dīn appeared in the world.
sentences.s583-s3: On the day of Hormazd a certain child was born to him, under a favorable star and an omen that lit the world.
sentences.s583-s4: At once the father named him Bahram; that child grew wise and became fortunate.
sentences.s583-s41: From Rûm and India, and from Persia — the astronomers; and if there are geometers among the people.
sentences.s583-s42: All philosophers, very learned, eloquent and (the) skilled people.
sentences.s583-s43: Each one spoke in a soft voice: 'O king, grant it — with counsel and modesty.'
sentences.s583-s44: We are wholly the dust beneath your feet; in matters of knowledge we are all your guides.
sentences.s583-s9: They fixed their gaze with firmness upon the star; from the Roman zīch as well they sought a path.
sentences.s583-s10: From the star he beheld so secretly joyful a one that he was a sovereign in the world.
sentences.s583-s11: He was king over the seven lands; say (announce) that he be joyfulhearted and pious.
sentences.s583-s12: The seekers went to the king, those same zīch and salābs at their side.
sentences.s583-s37: When the great men assembled in Pars, they came to (the) Yazdgerd (the crown-bearer).
sentences.s583-s38: They said: 'Whoever wishes that we be (his) servants, let him speak — we are poets.'
sentences.s583-s39: That such behaviour/time is required of guests as is fitting for the son of the king of the world.
sentences.s583-s40: He should take him up and teach him learning, and brighten his heart, driving it out of darkness.
sentences.s583-s17: When they departed from that court — the rad and the mobad and the king's pure dastur.
sentences.s583-s18: They sat and sought every kind of counsel, that so a remedy for whatever should come to pass be found.
sentences.s583-s19: If this little child does not take (on) his father's disposition, become a just, kingly ruler.
sentences.s583-s20: But if by chance he has his father's disposition, he will possess the whole realm, above and below.
